metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a default numeric value and a valid range (e.g. `0.0–1.0`, default `0.7`) for `overlap_conf_threshold` in the Scope config-key definition so executors can implement `from_env` and write deterministic threshold tests.
findings.finding: The `confidence` field on `overlap_verdict` is typed as `number` but no numeric range is ever defined, and `CONF_THRESHOLD` — which the aggregation rule applies as `confidence >= CONF_THRESHOLD` — has no stated default value anywhere in the plan. An executor must invent a number to implement `from_env` registration and the unit tests (which must wire a threshold to distinguish high- from low-confidence pairs).
findings.location: What section — `overlap_verdict` schema definition; Scope — `overlap_conf_threshold` as `overlap_*` key on `LLMConfig`; AC line: 'Abstain/low-confidence pairs are dropped'
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, update the `overlap_conf_threshold` description to: `overlap_conf_threshold` (float, range 0.0–1.0, default 0.7). Add a sentence: 'The `confidence` field on `overlap_verdict` is a float in [0.0, 1.0].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the formula for combining the two per-ordering confidence values into a single pair-level ranking scalar (e.g., `min(conf_AB, conf_BA)`) used for SURFACE_CAP ranking, and document it in both the aggregation rule and the output finding's `confidence` field definition.
findings.finding: When more than SURFACE_CAP pairs pass the dual-ordering filter, the plan says to surface 'highest-confidence results' — but each passing pair yields two per-ordering confidence values (one from A→B, one from B→A). The plan never specifies how these are combined into a single pair-level ranking scalar. Without a specified formula, different implementors will produce different SURFACE_CAP orderings, the output finding's `confidence` field is ambiguous, and the AC's 'highest-confidence' verification is unverifiable.
findings.location: What section — 'Surface at most SURFACE_CAP (default 3, configurable) highest-confidence results, each a finding {counterpart_id, relation, shared_artifact, confidence, link_command}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the aggregation rule in the What section: 'The surfaced finding's confidence = min(conf_A→B, conf_B→A); this conservative lower-bound is used for SURFACE_CAP ranking (ensuring the weakest ordering exceeds the threshold).' This is consistent with the fail-safe semantics of the rest of the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the combination rule for the two per-ordering confidence scalars (e.g. 'use the minimum of the two orderings' confidence values as the pair's final confidence for ranking') in the What section alongside the SURFACE_CAP description.
findings.finding: When both orderings of a pair pass the aggregation gate, the plan says to surface the 'highest-confidence' result — but each passing pair yields two per-ordering `confidence` scalars. The plan never specifies how those two values are combined for ranking (min, max, mean, or some other rule). This leaves the SURFACE_CAP ranking logic unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Surface at most SURFACE_CAP (default 3, configurable) highest-confidence results'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'For ranking, a pair's final confidence is the minimum of the two per-ordering confidence values (conservative aggregation). Pairs are ranked by this final confidence, highest first, before the SURFACE_CAP cut.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'the both-orderings instruction' from the prompt's MUST-encode list; replace it with a clear statement that the prompt should instruct the LLM to judge the presented pair (A, B) in the given order only, citing a shared artifact, without reference to any other call — the both-orderings aggregation is judge.py's responsibility, not the prompt's.
findings.finding: The Scope section mandates that `overlap_judge.md` body MUST encode 'the both-orderings instruction' — but the both-orderings aggregation is a CALLER-side operation in `judge.py` (run A→B, run B→A, compare). The LLM sees only ONE call per invocation and cannot observe the other ordering's result. Encoding a 'both-orderings instruction' in the prompt is a pink-elephant antipattern: it tells the LLM about a multi-call protocol it has no visibility into, which may cause the model to anticipate a second call, second-guess its output, or produce hedged responses, undermining the single-turn judgment.
findings.location: Scope — 'rubric prompt … whose body MUST encode: … the both-orderings instruction'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the both-orderings instruction' in the MUST-encode list with: 'judge the presented pair (query_ticket, candidate_ticket) in the order given; do not speculate about reverse orderings.' Document in judge.py's docstring that it calls the prompt twice with swapped inputs and aggregates results.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the eval AC, e.g., `rebar eval src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/overlap_judge.eval.yaml --assert precision>=0.8 recall>=0.6` (or the project's actual eval runner invocation), and state whether this runs against a live LLM or a deterministic fixture-replay so the command is reproducible in CI.
findings.finding: The eval AC ('Eval fixture ≥12 labeled pairs gives precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6') has no proving command — no invocation of a test runner, eval harness, or CLI tool is specified. There is no way to produce verifiable evidence that the threshold was met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, last bullet: 'Eval fixture (≥12 labeled pairs) gives precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete `category` value for `overlap_judge.md` to the Scope section (e.g. `category: overlap` if this is a new category, or confirm the correct existing value), and if introducing a new category, document where it must be registered.
findings.finding: The `overlap_judge.md` prompt front-matter specifies a `category` field (required by the framework — tool-verified: every existing reviewer prompt in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` has a `category` value, and `index.json` encodes it) but the plan never states what value to assign. For a novel operational judgment prompt (not a plan-review criterion and not a pass), no valid category is obvious, leaving the executor to guess or scan all existing prompts for a convention.
findings.location: Scope — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the labeled-pair fixture schema inline or by reference (e.g., `{query_digest, counterpart_digest, ground_truth_relation, ground_truth_shared_artifact}`) in the Testing section so the executor can create conforming fixtures without inventing a format.
findings.finding: The fixture format for `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` is described only as 'format documented' with no specification of what that documentation is, where it lives, or what schema each labeled pair must have (e.g., fields for query digest, counterpart digest, ground-truth relation, shared_artifact). An executor must invent the format, which is a deferred design decision.
findings.location: Testing section: 'a LABELED fixture (≥12 pairs at `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/`, format documented)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: 'Each fixture file is a YAML document with keys: `query_digest` (string), `counterpart_digest` (string), `ground_truth_relation` (one of the 5 enum values), `ground_truth_shared_artifact` (string|null). See `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/README.md` for examples.' If a project-wide fixture format already exists, cite it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact import path for `OverlapCandidate` (e.g. `from rebar.llm.overlap.retrieve import OverlapCandidate`) so the judge's dependency on S5 is unambiguous and testable once S5 ships; or mark this plan as blocked-by S5 explicitly.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate` does not exist anywhere in the codebase; it is declared as a dependency from S5 but S5 is explicitly out of scope for this plan. The plan treats it as a callable, importable interface without marking the dependency as blocking or identifying where in the codebase the symbol will live when S5 ships.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or tightly constrain the optional cascade pre-pass: either drop it entirely (K=20 / ≤40 calls needs no optimization) or specify an explicit safety guard — e.g., 'only prune if pre-pass returns unrelated with confidence ≥ HIGH_THRESHOLD AND the pair would not be escalated if a surfaceable relation were returned in either ordering' — to prevent silently discarding genuine duplicates.
findings.finding: The optional single-ordering cascade pre-pass reintroduces the exact position-bias failure mode the dual-ordering main pass was designed to eliminate: a genuinely duplicate pair that happens to look 'unrelated' in the A→B ordering will be pruned by the pre-pass and never reach the two-ordering confirm step, silently suppressing a real finding. The plan specifies no safety guard for this decision (no minimum pre-pass confidence, no 'escalate-on-any-surfaceable-label' rule). The plan's own K=20 figure (≤40 calls) supplies no scale evidence to justify the optimization complexity.
findings.location: What section — 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls.'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the 'Optional cascade' sentence from the What section. If call-cost optimization becomes necessary in a future iteration, add it with a profiling result and an explicit safety guard (e.g., only prune on unrelated with confidence ≥ 0.95 AND symmetric agreement on a second independent single-ordering call).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly mark the optional cascade as IN scope (with a config key and a test) or OUT of scope (with a note like 'deferred to a future stage') so the executor is not left to decide.
findings.finding: The 'optional cascade' (cheap single-ordering pre-pass that drops obvious `unrelated` pairs before the 2-ordering confirm) is mentioned in the Scope section without a clear in-scope/out-of-scope decision. No AC covers it, no test covers it, and no config key is assigned to it. An executor must decide whether to implement it, which is a deferred design choice.
findings.location: What section: 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the Scope OUT list: 'Optional cascade pre-pass (deferred; executor should NOT implement in this stage).' Or, if in scope, add a config key `overlap_cascade_enabled: bool = False` and a test `::test_cascade_drops_unrelated`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add Acceptance Criteria covering (a) `overlap_verdict` is registered in `contracts.py` (e.g., verified by import/schema-validation unit test) and (b) `overlap_enabled` is present in `VerifyConfig` and gates judge execution (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py::test_disabled_skips_judging`).
findings.finding: The Scope describes two additional deliverables — registration of `overlap_verdict` schema in `contracts.py` and the `overlap_enabled` gate flag in `VerifyConfig` — but no Acceptance Criterion covers either; these are described deliverables with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Scope ('overlap_verdict schema + contracts.py registration'; 'only the gate flag overlap_enabled lives in VerifyConfig') vs Acceptance Criteria (all 5 bullets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the rationale for the both-orderings aggregation rule and the position-bias failure-safe design decision.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage with a position-bias mitigation pattern (both-orderings aggregation rule) but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture this architectural decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## Scope / stage overview
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a `docs/adr/overlap-judge-ordering.md`) that records: (1) why both orderings are run, (2) the aggregation rule and its fail-safe direction, (3) the decision to restrict surfaceable relations to three, and reference it from the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured observability (at minimum: a counter for abstain events with reason label, a counter for surfaced findings, and a latency histogram per judge call) to judge.py, documented in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan's only observability for the new LLM integration point is 'logged' — no metric counter (abstain rate, error rate), latency histogram, or alert is planned for the 2K LLM calls per query, making it impossible for operators to detect a degraded or silently-abstaining judge.
findings.location: ## What — Error handling paragraph; ## Testing
findings.suggested_fix: Emit at least three metrics from judge.py: `overlap_judge.abstain_total{reason=timeout|error|malformed|low_conf}`, `overlap_judge.surfaced_total`, `overlap_judge.call_duration_seconds`; log a structured WARNING with candidate id and reason on every abstain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies (a) `overlap_verdict` is importable from `contracts.py` and round-trips through its schema validator, and (b) `overlap_judge.md` contains the required structural sections (4-way classification, shared-artifact gate, both-orderings instruction, abstain criterion) — e.g., via a lightweight prompt-lint test or a documentation-as-code check.
findings.finding: Two non-deferred deliverables named in Scope — (1) the `overlap_verdict` schema registration in `contracts.py` and (2) the rubric prompt file `overlap_judge.md` with its MUST-encode content requirements — have no corresponding Acceptance Criterion that provides an end-state-observable proof of their correctness.
findings.location: ## Scope (contracts.py registration; overlap_judge.md body); ## Acceptance Criteria (all five AC lines)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new AC: '`overlap_verdict` is registered in `contracts.py` and can be instantiated/validated (verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py::test_schema_roundtrip`); `overlap_judge.md` includes headings or markers for each MUST-encode section (verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py::test_prompt_structure`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` need full `LLMConfig`/`from_env` promotion (i.e., will ≥2 additional consumers read them), or whether module-level constants in `judge.py` (matching the `_DESC_CAP` / `DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS` pattern) are sufficient until a second call-site materialises.
findings.finding: The two new `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` keys are promoted to `LLMConfig` fields (registered in `from_env`, exposed in `[tool.rebar.llm]`) but have exactly one named call-site each: the new `judge.py`. No second or third consumer is cited in the plan or found in the codebase. The Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 existing call-sites before promoting to a shared config layer; the existing pattern for single-consumer thresholds is module-level constants (e.g., `_DESC_CAP = 600` in `spec_scan.py`, `DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS` in `config.py`).
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig` (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`, `[tool.rebar.llm]`, registered in `from_env`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `CONF_THRESHOLD: float = 0.7` and `SURFACE_CAP: int = 3` as module-level constants in `judge.py`; document them as the tuning knobs in a comment. Promote to `LLMConfig` only when a second consumer (e.g., a batch re-judge or a CLI flag) is added.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'optional cascade' pre-pass from the scope or gate it behind documented profiling evidence showing 40 calls/query is a demonstrated bottleneck; the simple 2K-call loop is already bounded and flat in corpus size.
findings.finding: The 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls' is a premature optimisation. The plan itself states K=20 (default from S5), meaning the bounded judge makes at most 40 LLM calls per query. No profiling result, latency measurement, or cost budget evidence is cited to justify the added complexity of a two-phase cascade.
findings.location: What section: 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the optional cascade from the Scope and What sections. If call cost becomes a concern post-launch, a spike with measured data can justify adding it then.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `OverlapCandidate` (and the query/corpus digest types it carries) is fully defined and importable from S5 before accepting this plan as ready-to-implement; if S5 is not yet shipped, add an explicit blocking dependency and define the minimal interface contract (field names, types) here so S6 can be implemented against a stub.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate` — the primary dependency the plan declares as 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests)' — does not exist anywhere in the codebase. A grep across all source files returns zero hits in src/. The plan treats it as a pre-existing, callable interface this stage can consume, but it is entirely absent.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests). Consumed by S7.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a minimal interface stub in this plan's Scope section, e.g.: `OverlapCandidate = TypedDict('OverlapCandidate', {'ticket_id': str, 'query_digest': dict, 'corpus_digest': dict})`, or add a hard 'BLOCKED ON S5 being merged' gate to the plan's Dependencies section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the list of files to create so the package is importable.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` is a new Python package directory, but the plan names only `judge.py` within it and omits the required `__init__.py`. Without `__init__.py` the directory is not importable as `rebar.llm.overlap` under standard Python packaging.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New `src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (may be empty or re-export the public judge API) to the Scope file list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` as an edit target (regenerate after adding `overlap_judge.md`) and note that `tests/unit/test_prompt_index.py::test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated` must pass.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` is an auto-generated file checked by `tests/unit/test_prompt_index.py::test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated`. Adding `overlap_judge.md` to the `reviewers/` directory will change the generated index; the plan does not enumerate regenerating `index.json` or the failing test as a consumer to address.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: after writing `overlap_judge.md`, run `rebar prompt build-index` (or the equivalent) to regenerate `index.json`, and commit the result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercer dict update in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as a required edit alongside adding `overlap_enabled` to `VerifyConfig`.
findings.finding: The plan says `overlap_enabled` lives only in `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`), but adding a field to `VerifyConfig` also requires updating the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercer dict in the same file — an edit target the plan does not enumerate.
findings.location: Scope section: 'only the gate flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'update `_SECTIONS["verify"]` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with a `bool` coercer entry for `overlap_enabled`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or unit test that asserts `contracts.response_model_for('overlap_verdict')` returns a model with the `relation`, `shared_artifact`, `confidence`, and `abstain` fields — verifying the registration is active before any judge call.
findings.finding: The `outputs: overlap_verdict` front-matter on `overlap_judge.md` is load-bearing: the framework's `response_model_for(output_schema)` in `contracts.py` silently falls back to the findings model when the contract name is not registered (tool-verified: `contracts.py:37-39`). The plan lists `overlap_verdict` schema registration in `contracts.py` as a Scope deliverable but no AC explicitly verifies that the contract IS registered before any call is made. A missing registration produces a silent wrong-schema call, not an error.
findings.location: Scope — 'overlap_verdict schema + contracts.py registration'; src/rebar/llm/contracts.py:37-39
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing: `test_contract_registered`: `from rebar.llm import contracts; m = contracts.response_model_for('overlap_verdict'); assert hasattr(m, 'relation') and hasattr(m, 'abstain')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly whether the eval runs against a live LLM endpoint or a deterministic fixture-replay, and if live, add an AC (even opt-in / manually run) that exercises the judge end-to-end against a real model at least once before the stage closes.
findings.finding: The eval specification does not state whether the judge runs against a live LLM or a deterministic fixture-replay when producing precision/recall measurements. If it requires live LLM calls, the plan has no AC that exercises the real LLM path end-to-end (S7 wiring is explicitly out of scope), leaving the only non-mocked verification of the judge's actual LLM behavior with no proving command in this stage.
findings.location: Testing section: 'Eval `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/overlap_judge.eval.yaml`'; Scope: 'OUT: retrieval (S5), wiring (S7)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies the `overlap_judge.md` prompt content encodes all four MUST items (e.g., an integration/snapshot test or a structured prompt-lint step that checks for the 4-way classification, shared-artifact gate, both-orderings instruction, and abstain criterion).
findings.finding: The Scope section mandates that the rubric prompt `overlap_judge.md` body MUST encode four specific items (4-way classification, require-a-cited-shared-artifact gate, both-orderings instruction, abstain-when-unsure criterion), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies these content requirements — this is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Scope (rubric prompt requirements) vs Acceptance Criteria (all 5 bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: "The rubric prompt `overlap_judge.md` contains all four required elements (4-way classification, shared-artifact gate, both-orderings instruction, abstain-when-unsure); verified by a prompt-content test or CI lint step (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py::test_prompt_content`)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a configurable per-call timeout (e.g., `overlap_call_timeout_s` in LLMConfig) and document how it is enforced in judge.py (e.g., passed to the LLM client or wrapped in asyncio.wait_for).
findings.finding: The plan states 'LLM timeout' triggers abstain but never specifies how timeouts are enforced or configured, leaving a risk that a hung LLM client blocks all 2K calls indefinitely with no enforcement mechanism.
findings.location: ## What — Error handling paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_call_timeout_s: float = 10.0` to LLMConfig; wrap each judge call in `asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=config.overlap_call_timeout_s)` and catch `asyncio.TimeoutError` → abstain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the new `LLMConfig` field declarations, their types, defaults, env-var names (e.g. `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD`), and any required new resolver helper (e.g. `_llm_float`) that `from_env` must call — none of these exist in the current `config.py`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` will be added as `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` and 'registered in `from_env`'. Reading the actual `LLMConfig` dataclass (lines 434–524) confirms the class has no `overlap_*` fields and `from_env` resolves no such keys. The config layer exists, but the plan treats the integration point as though these keys already have a home, when the integration pattern does not yet accommodate them.
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap as overlap_* keys on LLMConfig (src/rebar/llm/config.py, [tool.rebar.llm], registered in from_env)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as an explicit edit target in Scope, specifying the `overlap_enabled: bool = False` field on `VerifyConfig` AND the corresponding `_SECTIONS['verify']['overlap_enabled']` lambda coercer entry — neither exists today.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'only the gate flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig`'. Reading `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (lines 209–300) and the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercer dict (lines 421–430) confirms no `overlap_enabled` field or coercer entry exists. The plan treats `VerifyConfig` as already capable of hosting this flag, but neither the dataclass nor the coercer have been extended.
findings.location: Scope section: 'only the gate flag overlap_enabled lives in VerifyConfig'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Edit `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`: add `overlap_enabled: bool = False` to `VerifyConfig` dataclass AND `"overlap_enabled": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to `_SECTIONS["verify"]`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the dual-ordering swap-ordering technique as established LLM-as-judge prior art (Zheng et al. 2023 / MT-Bench) and explicitly justify the plan's specific design choices (binary agreement rule, no tie class, no CoT requirement) against the empirically documented approach.
findings.finding: The plan's core anti-bias mechanism — running both orderings of each pair and surfacing a relation only when both agree — is the established LLM-as-judge swap-ordering technique (Zheng et al. 2023, MT-Bench/Chatbot Arena), yet the plan does not acknowledge this lineage. Without citing the prior art, implementors cannot tell whether the plan's aggregation rule (strict both-must-agree, no tie-break, no chain-of-thought requirement) aligns with or deviates from the empirically calibrated approach, nor can they adopt documented improvements (e.g., calibration via CoT, handling the 'tie' output class, statistical confidence vs. binary agreement).
findings.location: What section — 'Both orderings (A→B, B→A) are run; AGGREGATION RULE: surface a relation ONLY if BOTH orderings agree…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prior Art' note in the What or Why section citing the swap-ordering technique and stating why the strict binary-agreement rule (vs. confidence-weighted voting or a 'tie' output class) is the chosen design for this corpus size and relation taxonomy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief differentiation from the existing scan_epics_for_spec / spec-alignment mechanism in the Why or Scope section, explaining why pairwise ticket-to-ticket judging is a distinct problem not addressable by the existing approach.
findings.finding: The codebase already contains scan_epics_for_spec (src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py) and the spec-alignment reviewer, which perform LLM-based overlap/coverage detection between epics and a spec. This is internal prior art for the same broad problem domain (detecting when two ticket-level artifacts overlap in scope). The plan introduces a pairwise ticket-to-ticket judge without acknowledging or differentiating from this existing mechanism, leaving open whether the two approaches are complementary, partially redundant, or could share infrastructure.
findings.location: Why section and Scope section — no mention of existing scan_epics_for_spec or spec-alignment
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence in the Why section: 'scan_epics_for_spec detects whether epics cover a spec (one-to-many, spec-anchored); this judge detects whether any two tickets in the corpus are duplicates/supersedes/depends_on (pairwise, corpus-wide) — a distinct problem requiring a different retrieval + judgment architecture.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
